title: Unit 23 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Relative Pronouns (Who, Which, That)

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 23: Relative Pronouns. These are the words that link ideas together. 'The man WHO called', 'The book THAT I read'. In Greek, we have one word that is the absolute lazy-student's dream... and another that is a formal masterpiece. I'm William, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... introduce us to the king of pronouns.
It is the most useful word in the whole language. It is the universal relative pronoun. It never declines, it never changes gender, and it never changes case.
Universal and indeclinable! So if I want to say 'The boy who sings', 'The girl who sings', and 'The children who sing'... I just use
Το παιδί που τραγουδάει. Ο άντρας που βλέπω. Τα παιδιά που έρχονται.
It works for people, things, subjects, and objects. It’s perfect.
'Που' is definitely the favorite. But... sometimes we see a much longer, more complicated pronoun.
is our formal relative pronoun. It behaves like an adjective. It MUST match its noun in gender, number, and case.
Ο άντρας ο οποίος... Η γυναίκα η οποία... Το παιδί το οποίο.
To be precise! Sometimes, if you have two nouns of different genders,
can be ambiguous. But if you use
the gender ending tells the listener exactly which noun you are referring to. It’s also used after prepositions. We say
is for precision and prepositions. Pro Tip: Stick to
for 95% of your life. Now, Athina... what about 'Whoever' or 'Whatever'?
It declines just like the regular one. 'Whatever' is
Μπορείς να μου δώσεις ό,τι έχεις;
... Can you give me whatever you have?
'Ό,τι'. This concludes Unit 23. You now have the glue to build complex sentences. Excellent work.
Μπράβο σας! Ο δρόμος είναι δικός σας. Γεια σας!
